items.md: him an omelet. The first assistant tells the second assistant, who tells Donald — but by this time, decorum has vanished, and Donald is addressed this way: "Hey, you! The prof wants an omelet! Get busy!" Donald relays the order to the nephews with even more bluster and arrogance. It adds up to a subtle, highly concentrated commentary on class distinctions, and how the people at the top are insulated from the dirty work below.

The prof wants an omelet! Get busy!" Donald relays the order to the nephews with even more bluster and arrogance. items.md: Usually, such episodes were like coal-black clouds in otherwise clear skies, but satire dominates one remarkable story, in the June 1951 *Walt Disney's Comics*. As might have been expected from someone with Barks's generally dim view of humanity, the satire is not directed at some social injustice, but is Swiftian in its scope.
items.value: Usually, such episodes were like coal-black clouds in otherwise clear skies, but satire dominates one remarkable story, in the June 1951 Walt Disney's Comics. items.md: In this story, Donald has replaced his troublesome garden with a swimming pool. As he dashes into the house to change his clothes for his first swim, two appealing small children ask timidly if they can have "one teeny weeny little swim in it?" Reluctantly, Donald agrees — and returns from his house a few minutes later to find that his pool has been seized by a
items.value: In this story, Donald has replaced his troublesome garden with a swimming pool. As he dashes into the house to change his clothes for his first swim, two appealing small children ask timidly if they can have "one teeny weeny little swim in it?" Reluctantly, Donald agrees — and returns from his house a few minutes later to find that his pool has been seized by a

items.md: horde of buck-toothed, freckle-faced little monsters. After two days of this, Donald in desperation has the pool filled with ice. Deprived of "their" pool, the children turn savage, driving Donald from the streets in a hail of eggs and tomatoes, and vow to return. The children, a thoroughly unsavory lot, are matched by their Amazon mothers, who storm Donald's house demanding that he install a lifeguard to protect their little darlings from sunburn and skinned knees or risk being tarred and feathered. At the close of the story, the nephews, who have been away to summer camp, return to find the pool filled in and once again planted as a garden.
items.value: horde of buck-toothed, freckle-faced little monsters. After two days of this, Donald in desperation has the pool filled with ice. Deprived of "their" pool, the children turn savage, driving Donald from the streets in a hail of eggs and tomatoes, and vow to return. The children, a thoroughly unsavory lot, are matched by their Amazon mothers, who storm Donald's house demanding that he install a lifeguard to protect their little darlings from sunburn and skinned knees or risk being tarred and feathered. At the close of the story, the nephews, who have been away to summer camp, return to find the pool filled in and once again planted as a garden.

items.md: Except for Donald, this story is filled with nothing but repulsive human specimens, mostly nasty, grasping suburban children and their hard, brass-tongued mothers. This in a comic book that was bought mainly by such suburban children and their parents. It's a little surprising it was ever printed — especially since Western's timorous editors shelved other stories that were no more biting than this one.
